_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against NCCN Head and Neck Cancers Guideline v2.2024, ASCO Larynx Preservation Update (Forastiere JCO 2018, PMID 29172863), RTOG 91-11 long-term results (Forastiere JCO 2013, PMID 23182993), the original Department of Veterans Affairs Laryngeal Cancer Study Group NEJM 1991 (PMID 11784875 / 2034244), SEER laryngeal cancer stat facts 2024, AJCC 8th edition TNM staging for larynx, and the ACS Laryngeal and Hypopharyngeal Cancer patient page. Numbers and drug regimens checked against NCCN, ASCO, and ENT/oncology UpToDate Lexicomp entries.

h1Title: Laryngeal Cancer: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Laryngeal cancer is a malignant tumour of the voice box, in over 95% of cases a squamous cell carcinoma arising from the lining of the glottis (the vocal cords), supraglottis, or subglottis. SEER estimates roughly 12,650 new diagnoses and 3,880 deaths per year in the United States, with a median age at diagnosis of 66 and a 4-to-1 male predominance. Persistent hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks is the dominant early sign of glottic disease; supraglottic tumours present later with a sore throat, ear pain, or a neck lump. Five-year relative survival is 61% overall but exceeds 80% when caught at the localised stage. Treatment combines transoral laser or robotic surgery, partial or total laryngectomy, radiation, and concurrent chemoradiation under NCCN-aligned multidisciplinary care.

keyStats.prevalence: ~12,650 new US cases and ~3,880 deaths per year (SEER 2024); ~184,615 people living with laryngeal cancer in the US
keyStats.demographics: Men diagnosed 4-5x more often than women; Black men have the highest US incidence and lowest 5-year survival
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 66; rare before 40, peak incidence 60-80
keyStats.globalCases: ~189,000 new cases and ~103,000 deaths globally per year (GLOBOCAN 2022)

definition: Laryngeal cancer (ICD-10: C32) is a malignant epithelial tumour of the larynx, the cartilaginous voice organ between the pharynx and the trachea. Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for over 95% of cases and is anatomically divided into three subsites with very different biology: glottic cancer (true vocal cords; roughly 60% of cases) presents earliest because even a small lesion changes the voice, supraglottic cancer (epiglottis, false cords, aryepiglottic folds; roughly 30-35%) is associated with heavy alcohol use and lymph node spread, and subglottic cancer (below the cords; under 5%) often presents late with airway obstruction. Field cancerisation from inhaled tobacco carcinogens means second primary tumours of the lung, oral cavity, oropharynx, or oesophagus occur in 10-25% of patients over a lifetime. Staging follows the AJCC 8th edition TNM system, which incorporates depth of invasion, cartilage involvement, vocal cord fixation, and regional nodal disease. Laryngeal cancer is managed by an ENT (otolaryngology - head and neck surgery) team in partnership with radiation oncology, medical oncology, speech and language pathology, and rehabilitation, almost always in a multidisciplinary tumour board.

typesClassification.description: Arises on the true vocal cords. Hoarseness appears with millimetre-scale lesions, so a high proportion are diagnosed at T1-T2 (stage I-II). The glottis has sparse lymphatic drainage, so nodal spread is uncommon until T3-T4. Five-year survival exceeds 80% for early-stage glottic disease.

Arises from the epiglottis, false cords, aryepiglottic folds, or arytenoids. Rich bilateral lymphatic drainage produces neck nodal metastasis in 25-50% of patients at diagnosis. Symptoms (sore throat, otalgia, dysphagia) are non-specific so disease is often advanced at presentation.

Arises below the vocal cords. Rare, usually presents with stridor or airway obstruction when the lesion is large, and frequently involves the cricoid cartilage and paratracheal nodes. Treatment often requires total laryngectomy and adjuvant therapy.

An exophytic well-differentiated SCC variant accounting for 1-3% of laryngeal cancers. Locally destructive but rarely metastasises; treated primarily with surgery, with radiation reserved for unresectable disease.

Rare histologies include neuroendocrine carcinoma (large-cell and small-cell), minor salivary gland tumours (adenoid cystic carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma), chondrosarcoma of the cricoid, lymphoma, and metastatic deposits. Each has distinct treatment pathways and prognoses.

primarySymptoms: Persistent hoarseness or voice change lasting more than 3 weeks — the cardinal symptom of glottic cancer, present in over 90% of glottic cases at diagnosis and the trigger for ENT referral in international guidelines.
Sore throat that does not resolve over several weeks, often unilateral and worse on swallowing, typical of supraglottic primary tumours.
Referred ear pain (otalgia) without otoscopic findings, transmitted via the vagus nerve from a supraglottic or hypopharyngeal lesion — a classic red flag in head and neck oncology.
A painless lump in the neck representing metastatic cervical lymphadenopathy, most often the level II/III chain, frequently the presenting sign in supraglottic disease.
Progressive difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) for solids, sometimes with food sticking sensation, indicating a bulky supraglottic or post-cricoid lesion.
Noisy breathing (stridor) on inspiration or biphasic stridor at rest — a late sign indicating airway compromise that warrants urgent assessment.
Chronic dry cough or recurrent throat clearing, sometimes with blood-tinged sputum (haemoptysis) when the tumour ulcerates.
Unintentional weight loss of more than 5% of body weight over 3-6 months, reflecting cachexia, dysphagia, or pain with eating.
Foul-smelling breath (halitosis) from tumour necrosis, particularly in advanced ulcerated lesions.
Aspiration on liquids or solids in supraglottic disease, where tumour bulk and reduced laryngeal sensation impair the swallowing reflex.
earlyWarningSigns: Hoarseness lasting longer than 3 weeks in any adult, especially a current or former smoker — refer to ENT for laryngoscopy under the NICE 2-week-wait pathway
New unexplained otalgia in an adult with normal otoscopy — examine the larynx and pharynx
A neck lump persisting beyond 3 weeks in an adult over 45 — image with ultrasound-guided FNA and consult head and neck cancer team
Chronic sore throat or globus sensation in a smoker or heavy drinker, with no clear infective cause
Throat discomfort that worsens rather than improves over a 4-6 week course of reflux therapy
emergencySigns: Stridor at rest, drooling, inability to lie flat, or use of accessory respiratory muscles — impending airway obstruction requiring same-day ENT and anaesthetic assessment
Sudden bright red bleeding from the throat or mouth (haemoptysis or haematemesis) — possible tumour erosion into a vessel
Acute severe dysphagia with inability to swallow saliva — urgent endoscopy and imaging to exclude obstruction or aspiration risk
Carotid blowout: sudden expanding neck swelling or pulsatile bleeding in a patient with prior neck irradiation or surgery — life-threatening, immediate emergency surgery
New facial swelling with venous distension (superior vena cava syndrome features) — suggests bulky mediastinal nodal disease in advanced or metastatic disease

causes.description: Cigarette smoke is the single dominant cause, responsible for 70-80% of laryngeal cancer. Carcinogens (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, nitrosamines, aldehydes) bind to laryngeal epithelial DNA, drive TP53 and CDKN2A mutations, and produce field cancerisation. Risk rises with pack-years; heavy smokers carry a 10-20-fold elevation over never-smokers.

Ethanol and its acetaldehyde metabolite damage laryngeal mucosa directly and act synergistically with tobacco. Drinking more than 4 standard drinks daily multiplies tobacco-related risk by a further 2-3 fold, with the strongest effect on supraglottic carcinoma. Combined heavy use accounts for over 90% of cases in many cohorts.

causes.cause: Squamous epithelial dysplasia and field cancerisation
causes.description: Prolonged carcinogen exposure transforms the laryngeal lining through hyperplasia, mild, moderate, and severe dysplasia, then carcinoma in situ. Severe dysplasia progresses to invasive cancer in roughly 30% of patients over 5 years. The whole upper aerodigestive lining is at risk, explaining second primaries.

Unlike oropharyngeal SCC, where HPV-16 drives over 70% of new cases, HPV is identified in only 5-15% of laryngeal cancers and the prognostic benefit seen in oropharynx does not consistently apply to larynx. Recurrent respiratory papillomatosis caused by HPV-6/11 carries a small but real risk of malignant transformation.

Asbestos exposure (shipyard, construction, insulation), wood and metal dust, mustard gas, sulphuric acid mist, and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (coke ovens, diesel exhaust) are IARC Group 1 carcinogens for the larynx. Occupational exposure roughly doubles risk independent of smoking.

Long-standing acid and pepsin exposure may act as a co-carcinogen, particularly in non-smokers. Evidence is mixed — meta-analyses estimate a 2-3-fold increased risk, strongest for supraglottic disease — but reflux remains an under-recognised modifiable factor.

Polymorphisms in alcohol-metabolising enzymes (ADH1B, ALDH2) and DNA-repair genes (XPD, XRCC1) alter individual risk. Fanconi anaemia patients carry a 500-700-fold increased risk of head and neck SCC, often presenting in early adulthood.

riskFactors.description: Doubles risk within 5 years of starting and rises with pack-years. Heavy smokers (more than 25 cigarettes daily) carry a 10-20-fold risk increase. Risk halves within 5-10 years of cessation and approaches baseline after 15-20 years smoke-free.

riskFactors.factor: Heavy alcohol use (more than 4 drinks daily)
riskFactors.description: Independent and synergistic with tobacco. Combined heavy smoking plus heavy drinking can multiply risk 35-fold versus those who do neither. Effect is most pronounced for supraglottic cancer.
Men account for 75-80% of cases globally. The gap is closing in countries where female smoking rose in recent decades.
Median age at diagnosis is 66; over 90% of cases occur after age 55. Risk reflects cumulative tobacco and alcohol exposure plus age-related decline in DNA repair.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure (asbestos, wood dust, PAHs, sulphuric acid mist)
riskFactors.description: Asbestos exposure roughly doubles laryngeal cancer risk and is recognised as an occupational disease in many jurisdictions. Coke oven workers, painters, and shipyard workers have measurable excess risk.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and limited dietary fruit/vegetable intake
riskFactors.description: Low intake of fruits, vegetables, and antioxidants associates with a 30-50% increase in laryngeal cancer risk; socioeconomic disadvantage compounds smoking and alcohol exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Prior head and neck cancer or precancerous laryngeal lesion
riskFactors.description: Patients treated for prior head and neck SCC carry a 3-7% per year risk of a second primary in the aerodigestive tract. Severe laryngeal dysplasia progresses to invasive cancer in roughly 30% of patients within 5 years without treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Fanconi anaemia and other inherited DNA-repair disorders
riskFactors.description: Fanconi anaemia raises head and neck SCC risk 500-700-fold; cancers often appear before age 40 and dictate avoidance of standard radiation and platinum chemotherapy doses.
Long-standing acid and pepsin exposure roughly doubles laryngeal cancer risk in observational data, particularly in non-smoking patients with supraglottic disease.

HPV-16 is found in 5-15% of laryngeal cancers; the prognostic benefit seen in HPV-driven oropharyngeal cancer does not consistently apply. Recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (HPV-6/11) carries a small malignant transformation risk.

affectedDemographics: US incidence is 4-5x higher in men than women; Black men carry the highest incidence and lowest 5-year survival in SEER data
Highest global incidence is in eastern and southern Europe, parts of South America, and South Asia, mirroring tobacco and alcohol consumption patterns (GLOBOCAN 2022)
Incidence in high-income countries has fallen 2-3% per year over two decades, tracking declining male smoking rates; female incidence is stable or rising
Indian-subcontinent populations have an elevated burden linked to bidi smoking, smokeless tobacco (gutka, paan), and reverse smoking, with younger ages at diagnosis
Pediatric laryngeal cancer is exceedingly rare and usually signals malignant transformation of recurrent respiratory papillomatosis or an underlying syndrome such as Fanconi anaemia
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is built on a focused history, full head and neck examination, flexible laryngoscopy in clinic, and tissue biopsy obtained under direct laryngoscopy. NICE NG12 and the American Head & Neck Society recommend urgent ENT referral for any adult with hoarseness lasting over 3 weeks, especially current or former smokers, and any persistent neck lump beyond 3 weeks in adults over 45. Flexible nasendoscopy at the bedside characterises tumour location, extent across subsites, vocal cord mobility (a key staging variable), and airway adequacy. Stroboscopy adds information on mucosal wave and is useful for early glottic lesions. Definitive tissue diagnosis requires biopsy under general anaesthesia at panendoscopy, which simultaneously maps the tumour, screens for synchronous second primaries in the upper aerodigestive tract (occurring in 5-10% of patients), and tattoos the lesion for treatment planning. Cross-sectional imaging — contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest, or MRI for soft-tissue and cartilage detail — defines T-stage by documenting cartilage invasion, preepiglottic and paraglottic space involvement, and pre-vertebral extension. Neck ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration evaluates nodal status; FDG-PET/CT is recommended for stage III-IV disease to detect distant metastasis and unknown primaries. Pulmonary function tests and a swallowing assessment are obtained when partial laryngectomy or radiotherapy is being considered. Staging follows the AJCC 8th edition TNM system, and every case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary head and neck oncology meeting before treatment is offered.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line in-office examination of the larynx. Visualises the tumour, defines its subsite (glottic, supraglottic, subglottic), assesses vocal cord mobility (T-stage determinant), and checks airway patency. Findings drive urgency of operative endoscopy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After local anaesthetic spray to the nose, a thin flexible camera is passed through one nostril to the throat. The exam takes 1-2 minutes; the patient sits upright, breathes normally, and may be asked to speak or hold an 'eee' sound. Mild gagging is common but the procedure is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Panendoscopy with biopsy under general anaesthesia
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard tissue diagnosis. Direct laryngoscopy, pharyngoscopy, and oesophagoscopy in one procedure map the lesion, take biopsies, screen for synchronous second primaries, and allow palpation of disease extent. Often combined with transoral laser excision for small T1 lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case general anaesthetic. The surgeon uses rigid endoscopes through the mouth; biopsies are taken with cup forceps or laser. Sore throat for 3-5 days afterwards is normal; results return 5-10 days later.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines T-stage by showing cartilage invasion, preepiglottic and paraglottic space involvement, and nodal disease. Chest imaging screens for lung metastases and the synchronous lung primary common in heavy smokers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10-minute scan with intravenous iodinated contrast through a forearm cannula. The contrast can produce a brief warm flush. Renal function is checked beforehand. Results are read by a head and neck radiologist usually within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the larynx and neck
diagnosticTests.purpose: Best soft-tissue resolution for assessing cartilage invasion, perineural spread, and base-of-tongue extension when CT is equivocal. Particularly useful before partial laryngectomy planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes inside a magnet, lying still on the back with a coil placed over the neck. Loud knocking noise is normal; ear protection is provided. Intravenous gadolinium contrast is used in most protocols.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Recommended for stage III-IV disease (NCCN). Detects distant metastases, unknown primary tumours, and post-treatment residual disease. A 12-week post-radiotherapy PET/CT with low metabolic activity has a high negative predictive value and can avoid planned neck dissection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Inject radiotracer FDG and rest quietly for 60 minutes. Scan takes 20-30 minutes. Fasting 6 hours and good glycaemic control are required for diagnostic-quality images.

diagnosticTests.test: Neck ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration cytology (US-FNAC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates suspicious cervical lymph nodes. Sensitivity for nodal metastasis is roughly 80-90% in experienced hands; FNA confirms histology before treatment planning, especially when the primary is small.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A radiologist or head and neck surgeon scans the neck with a probe and gel, then passes a fine needle into the suspect node under ultrasound guidance. Mild discomfort, no sedation, results typically within 2-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Heavy smokers commonly have coexistent COPD. PFTs inform fitness for surgery, radiation tolerance, and post-laryngectomy rehabilitation planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Spirometry and lung volume measurements taking 20-30 minutes. The patient blows into a mouthpiece following technician instructions; no sedation or radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: Modified barium swallow and FEES (fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses baseline swallowing function before treatment, especially in supraglottic disease and before partial laryngectomy or radiotherapy. Identifies silent aspiration that predicts post-treatment complications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient swallows barium-coated foods of different consistencies under fluoroscopy (modified barium swallow), or coloured boluses while a flexible camera observes the larynx (FEES). Both are 15-30 minutes; painless.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of laryngeal cancer is multidisciplinary and stage-driven, prioritising oncological cure together with preservation of voice, swallowing, and airway whenever possible. The NCCN Head and Neck Cancers guideline and the ASCO Larynx Preservation Update (Forastiere JCO 2018) provide the operational framework. Early-stage glottic disease (T1-T2 N0) is treated with single-modality therapy — either transoral laser microsurgery (TLM), transoral robotic surgery (TORS), or definitive radiation therapy — with equivalent local control rates of 85-95% and a focus on voice quality outcomes. Early supraglottic disease often requires elective neck irradiation or selective neck dissection because of the 25-50% occult nodal metastasis rate. Locally advanced disease (T3-T4 or N+) requires multi-modality treatment. Three options exist: (1) concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation (cisplatin 100 mg/m² every 3 weeks plus 70 Gy radiation), which gives the best laryngeal preservation rate (84% at 2 years and 67% at 10 years in RTOG 91-11; Forastiere NEJM 2003 and JCO 2013); (2) induction chemotherapy with docetaxel-cisplatin-5FU followed by radiation or chemoradiation for responders, reserving total laryngectomy for non-responders; or (3) upfront total laryngectomy with adjuvant radiotherapy (plus concurrent cisplatin if positive margins or extranodal extension; Bernier/Cooper NEJM 2004). T4a tumours with bulky cartilage destruction or laryngeal dysfunction are best served by total laryngectomy because functional outcomes after non-surgical organ preservation in this group are poor. Targeted therapy with cetuximab plus radiation is an alternative in cisplatin-ineligible patients (Bonner NEJM 2006). Recurrent and metastatic disease is managed with immune checkpoint inhibitors — pembrolizumab monotherapy or pembrolizumab plus platinum-5FU is now first-line based on KEYNOTE-048 (Burtness Lancet 2019). Speech and language therapy is involved from diagnosis through rehabilitation; smoking cessation during and after treatment improves response rates and survival. Every plan is built around a written tumour board recommendation, with the patient choosing between equivalent options when they exist.

medicalTreatments.name: Definitive radiation therapy (66-70 Gy, ~2 Gy per fraction, 6-7 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Primary treatment for T1-T2 glottic and supraglottic cancers and a non-surgical option for locally advanced disease combined with chemotherapy. Modern IMRT (intensity-modulated radiation therapy) spares the parotid glands and uninvolved larynx, reducing xerostomia and dysphagia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year local control 85-95% for T1 glottic, 70-85% for T2 glottic. Larynx preservation rate is approximately 85% in T1-T2 disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Concurrent cisplatin chemoradiation (cisplatin 100 mg/m² IV on days 1, 22, 43 with 70 Gy radiation)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard of care for locally advanced laryngeal cancer (stage III-IVa) when larynx preservation is the goal. Demonstrated superior larynx preservation versus induction chemotherapy or radiation alone in RTOG 91-11 (Forastiere NEJM 2003; long-term JCO 2013). Acute mucositis, dysphagia, and weight loss are universal.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 2-year larynx preservation 88%, 10-year 67% (RTOG 91-11). Locoregional control 78% at 2 years; overall survival broadly similar to total laryngectomy with adjuvant therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Induction (neoadjuvant) chemotherapy with docetaxel-cisplatin-fluorouracil (TPF), followed by radiation
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for advanced disease where larynx preservation is desired but bulky tumours may not respond to chemoradiation alone. Used to triage responders to radiation and non-responders to total laryngectomy. Increases neutropenia and toxicity versus cisplatin-radiation; not superior in larynx preservation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate roughly 70-80% after 2-3 cycles; long-term larynx preservation 50-60%. TPF superior to PF induction (Pointreau JNCI 2009, GORTEC 2000-01).

medicalTreatments.name: Cetuximab plus radiation (cetuximab 400 mg/m² loading then 250 mg/m² weekly with 70 Gy radiation)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative bioradiation for patients who cannot tolerate cisplatin (renal impairment, ototoxicity, frailty). Targets the EGFR pathway; main toxicity is an acneiform rash and infusion reactions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Locoregional control improved by approximately 10% versus radiation alone, with median overall survival of 49 vs 29 months in the Bonner trial (NEJM 2006). Less effective than cisplatin-radiation in head-to-head data.

medicalTreatments.name: Pembrolizumab (200 mg IV every 3 weeks) — alone or with platinum-fluorouracil
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for recurrent or metastatic head and neck SCC based on KEYNOTE-048 (Burtness Lancet 2019). Pembrolizumab monotherapy is used when PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) ≥1; combination with platinum-5FU is offered regardless of CPS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival of approximately 14.9 months with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus 10.7 months with cetuximab-chemotherapy in CPS ≥1 disease. Response rates 23-36% depending on biomarker and regimen.

medicalTreatments.name: Nivolumab (240 mg IV every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Approved for platinum-refractory recurrent or metastatic head and neck SCC after CheckMate 141 (Ferris NEJM 2016). Second-line option when disease progresses within 6 months of platinum-based therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median overall survival 7.5 vs 5.1 months versus investigator's choice chemotherapy; 1-year survival 36% vs 17% (CheckMate 141).

medicalTreatments.name: Adjuvant radiation therapy (60-66 Gy over 6-7 weeks) after surgery
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated after total or partial laryngectomy when high-risk features are present: positive or close margins, perineural or lymphovascular invasion, multiple positive nodes, or pT3-T4 disease. Started within 6 weeks of surgery for best local control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces locoregional recurrence by 20-30% in high-risk disease. When combined with concurrent cisplatin for positive margins or extranodal extension, locoregional control and disease-free survival improve further (Bernier/Cooper NEJM 2004).

medicalTreatments.name: Best supportive and palliative care with airway management
medicalTreatments.description: For unfit patients or those who decline aggressive therapy, palliation focuses on airway (tracheostomy), nutrition (PEG feeding), pain control, palliative radiotherapy for symptom relief, and access to head and neck specialist nursing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves symptom burden, quality of life, and time at home; median survival 6-12 months for metastatic disease on best supportive care, longer with palliative systemic therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Transoral laser microsurgery (TLM) or transoral robotic surgery (TORS)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic excision of early laryngeal cancers (Tis, T1, selected T2) using CO2 laser or robotic arms through the mouth. Avoids external incisions, preserves the larynx, and shortens recovery to days rather than weeks. Voice quality is good but slightly inferior to radiation for some T1a lesions.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Local control 85-95% for T1 glottic disease; salvage options remain intact if recurrence occurs. Day-case or short-stay surgery in most centres.

surgicalOptions.name: Partial laryngectomy (vertical hemilaryngectomy, supracricoid partial laryngectomy, supraglottic laryngectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Open surgical removal of part of the larynx with preservation of voice and airway. Selected for T2-T3 tumours where the entire larynx is not involved. Requires careful patient selection — adequate lung function and motivation for swallowing rehabilitation are essential.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year local control 80-90% in suitable patients; aspiration and dysphagia for several weeks after surgery resolves with rehabilitation in most cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Total laryngectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the entire larynx with creation of a permanent stoma (tracheostome) in the neck. Standard for T4a tumours with cartilage destruction, dysfunctional larynx, or after failed organ preservation. Voice rehabilitation with tracheoesophageal puncture and voice prosthesis is the gold standard.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year disease-specific survival 60-70% for T3-T4a primaries treated with surgery plus adjuvant therapy. Functional voice with tracheoesophageal prosthesis achieved in 80-90% of motivated patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Neck dissection (selective, modified radical, or radical)
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of cervical lymph nodes for staging and therapy. Selective neck dissection (levels II-IV) is standard for supraglottic and advanced glottic cancers; modified radical dissection preserves the spinal accessory nerve, internal jugular vein, and sternocleidomastoid muscle where oncologically safe.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Regional control 85-95% when combined with primary site therapy; functional and cosmetic outcomes far superior with selective and modified radical approaches than classical radical dissection.

surgicalOptions.name: Salvage laryngectomy after failed radiation or chemoradiation
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when local recurrence develops after non-surgical larynx-preserving therapy, occurring in 15-30% of patients within 5 years. Higher complication rate (pharyngocutaneous fistula in 20-40%) than primary surgery, but offers a curative option.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year overall survival 30-50% after salvage laryngectomy; outcomes are better when salvage is performed for an isolated local recurrence within 2 years of initial therapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation counselling and nicotine replacement during and after treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: Continued smoking during radiotherapy reduces response rates by 30-40% and roughly doubles the risk of a second primary cancer (Browman NEJM 1993). Smoking cessation is the highest-yield 'alternative' intervention available and should be offered formally at every visit.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support and prophylactic PEG feeding in high-risk patients
alternativeTreatments.description: Severe mucositis and dysphagia during chemoradiation lead to 5-10% weight loss in over half of patients. Pre-emptive nutritional review with dietitian-led oral supplementation and prophylactic gastrostomy in selected cases reduces unplanned hospital admissions and treatment interruption (Paccagnella Support Care Cancer 2010).

whySeeSpecialist: Any adult with hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks, persistent unilateral sore throat, referred otalgia with a normal ear exam, a neck lump, or stridor should be referred urgently to ENT (otolaryngology - head and neck surgery), typically through a national 2-week-wait or fast-track pathway. Confirmed laryngeal cancer is managed at a head and neck oncology multidisciplinary centre with surgical, radiation, medical oncology, speech therapy, dietetic, and reconstructive expertise on site. Self-referral or generalist management is associated with worse stage at diagnosis and inferior survival.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an ENT consultant or head and neck oncology team that operates within a high-volume multidisciplinary centre (NCCN, ASCO, and NICE all recommend ≥30-50 head and neck cancers per year per centre as a quality threshold). Ask about transoral laser and robotic surgery availability, voice rehabilitation services, tracheoesophageal prosthesis programmes, and the proportion of patients discussed in a documented tumour board. Continuity matters: the same team should follow you through diagnosis, treatment, and 5-year surveillance.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking permanently — the single most powerful prevention strategy. Risk halves within 5-10 years and approaches baseline at 15-20 years smoke-free; combine with behavioural support and nicotine replacement or varenicline

Limit alcohol to no more than one drink per day for women and two for men — heavy drinkers carry a 2-3-fold increase in risk independent of tobacco, and the combination multiplies risk synergistically
Avoid smokeless tobacco, bidis, gutka, and paan — these products carry comparable or higher laryngeal cancer risk than cigarettes in South Asian populations
Use respiratory protection in occupations with asbestos, wood dust, sulphuric acid mist, or polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons; pursue annual occupational surveillance where indicated
Eat a diet rich in fruit, vegetables, and whole grains — low antioxidant intake is associated with a 30-50% increase in laryngeal cancer risk
Treat chronic laryngopharyngeal reflux with lifestyle measures, weight loss, and acid suppression where indicated
lifestyleModifications: Quit smoking and avoid second-hand smoke entirely once diagnosed — continued smoking during radiation reduces response and survival and increases second primary risk
Stop or substantially reduce alcohol intake during and after treatment to support mucosal healing and reduce second primary risk
Maintain dental and oral hygiene with twice-daily brushing, dental review before radiation, fluoride trays, and prompt management of mucositis to reduce osteoradionecrosis risk
Engage early with speech and language therapy — pre-treatment swallowing exercises preserve function during radiotherapy
Eat small frequent high-calorie meals and use prescribed oral nutritional supplements; expect 5-10% weight loss during chemoradiation
Stay physically active during and after treatment — supervised exercise reduces fatigue and improves treatment completion rates
Address mood and anxiety actively — head and neck cancer carries the highest suicide rate of any major cancer and benefits from early psychological support

recommended: 5+ daily servings of fruits and vegetables — protective effect observed in head and neck cancer cohorts
Soft, high-calorie, high-protein meals during radiation-induced mucositis (smoothies, scrambled eggs, oatmeal, mashed potatoes)
Oral nutritional supplements (1.5-2 kcal/mL drinks) when oral intake drops below 60% of needs
Adequate hydration (2-3 litres daily during radiotherapy) to mitigate xerostomia and mucositis
Lean protein at every meal (fish, eggs, dairy, legumes) to support tissue repair and maintain muscle mass
Foods rich in zinc and vitamin A (fortified cereals, dairy, leafy greens) — emerging evidence supports mucosal healing
avoid: All tobacco products — smoked, smokeless, bidis, gutka, paan, vape — there is no safe level during or after treatment
Alcohol during treatment and ideally permanently, especially spirits and beer that directly irritate inflamed mucosa
Spicy, acidic, very hot, or rough-textured foods during radiation-induced mucositis (citrus, tomato sauces, crisps, toast)
Heavily processed and high-salt foods that worsen xerostomia and dehydration
Sugary drinks and sweets that promote post-radiation dental caries when xerostomia is present
Crash diets and unprescribed restrictive regimens — weight loss during treatment is detrimental

exerciseGuidelines: Supervised aerobic and resistance exercise during and after head and neck cancer treatment improves fatigue, quality of life, and treatment completion rates. Aim for 150 minutes of moderate-intensity activity per week as tolerated, including 2-3 resistance sessions targeting major muscle groups. Activity should be reduced during acute mucositis or severe fatigue but not stopped entirely. Patients with a tracheostomy or laryngectomy can swim with a Larkel device and otherwise exercise freely with stoma protection in dusty or wet environments.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed analgesia, mouth rinses, and any antifungals or antibiotics consistently during radiotherapy — pain control protects nutritional intake
Inspect the neck and oral cavity weekly during follow-up for new lumps, ulcers, or skin changes; report any persistent change within 2 weeks
Perform stoma and prosthesis care daily after laryngectomy — humidification, suction as needed, and prosthesis cleaning per the speech therapist's regimen
Use a heat and moisture exchanger (HME) over the stoma to maintain airway humidification and reduce mucus crusting
Attend speech, swallowing, and lymphedema therapy sessions consistently — adherence is the strongest predictor of functional recovery
Keep all surveillance appointments — most recurrences occur within 2 years of treatment and many are detected at scheduled follow-up rather than by patient symptoms
prognosis: Prognosis depends primarily on stage, subsite, and treatment compliance. Five-year relative survival in SEER data is approximately 61% overall, 78% for localised disease, 47% for regional disease, and 34% for distant disease. Glottic cancers carry the best stage-for-stage prognosis because they present early; localised T1 glottic disease has a 5-year survival above 90% with either surgery or radiation. Supraglottic and subglottic primaries do worse due to later presentation and higher nodal involvement. After RTOG 91-11, locally advanced T3 laryngeal cancers treated with concurrent chemoradiation have 10-year overall survival of approximately 28% with 67% larynx preservation; outcomes after total laryngectomy plus adjuvant therapy are broadly similar in survival but commit the patient to a permanent stoma. The two largest modifiable prognostic factors are continued smoking, which roughly doubles the risk of treatment failure and second primaries, and treatment interruption from toxicity or non-compliance. Second primary tumours of the lung, oral cavity, oropharynx, and oesophagus occur in 10-25% of patients over a lifetime and account for many late deaths; lifelong surveillance with cross-sectional imaging and endoscopy is therefore standard.
recoveryTimeline: Recovery depends on treatment modality. Transoral laser surgery for T1 glottic disease produces 1-2 weeks of mild throat discomfort and full voice recovery within 4-8 weeks. Definitive radiotherapy carries 6-8 weeks of treatment with peak mucositis in weeks 4-6 and gradual recovery over 8-12 weeks; xerostomia, dysphagia, and fatigue can persist beyond 6 months. Concurrent chemoradiation adds neutropenia, ototoxicity, and renal effects; nutritional recovery often takes 6-12 months. Total laryngectomy requires 7-10 days in hospital, voice prosthesis training within 2-4 weeks, and 3-6 months of speech and swallowing rehabilitation. Surveillance every 2-3 months in year 1, every 3-4 months in year 2, every 4-6 months in years 3-5, and annually thereafter follows the NCCN schedule.
complications: Treatment-related dysphagia and aspiration — chronic in 20-40% after chemoradiation or partial laryngectomy; managed with speech therapy and, in severe cases, PEG feeding
Xerostomia and dental decay after radiotherapy — preventable with dental review before treatment, fluoride trays, and salivary substitutes
Osteoradionecrosis of the mandible — occurs in 2-7% of irradiated patients, more common after dental extractions in irradiated bone; managed with conservative care, hyperbaric oxygen, or surgery
Pharyngocutaneous fistula after total laryngectomy — occurs in 10-30% of cases, especially after salvage surgery in irradiated necks; most close with conservative management
Hypothyroidism after neck irradiation — develops in up to 50% of patients within 5 years; check TSH annually and treat with levothyroxine
Second primary cancers of the lung, oral cavity, oropharynx, and oesophagus — annual incidence of 3-7% per year over a lifetime; lifelong surveillance and smoking cessation are essential
Carotid blowout syndrome after irradiation or neck surgery — rare (~3% in re-irradiated patients) but often fatal; sentinel bleeds require urgent endovascular intervention
Psychosocial morbidity including depression, anxiety, social isolation, and elevated suicide risk — the highest of any major cancer; routine screening and referral are recommended

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute (NCI) — Laryngeal Cancer Treatment (PDQ)
supportResources.description: NCI patient-facing summary of stage-by-stage treatment, updated by an expert editorial board.

supportResources.name: Head and Neck Cancer Alliance
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organisation with peer support, financial assistance information, and survivor stories.

supportResources.name: Macmillan Cancer Support — Laryngeal Cancer (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing nurse helpline, benefits advice, and downloadable patient guides.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Laryngitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic laryngitis produces a fluctuating hoarseness lasting months that improves with vocal rest, hydration, and reflux therapy. Laryngeal cancer causes progressively worsening hoarseness over weeks that does not improve and is often unilateral on laryngoscopy. Any hoarseness persisting beyond 3 weeks in an adult smoker mandates urgent ENT laryngoscopy to exclude malignancy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Nodules
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord nodules are bilateral symmetrical thickenings at the junction of the anterior and middle third of the vocal folds, caused by voice misuse — typical in teachers, singers, and children. They improve with voice therapy alone. Cancer is usually unilateral, ulcerated or exophytic, fixed, and progressive, and never improves with voice therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal Cord Paralysis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord paralysis presents with a breathy weak voice from a fixed cord in the paramedian or lateral position, most often after thyroid or cardiothoracic surgery or from a recurrent laryngeal nerve lesion. The cord itself is smooth and non-tumoural on laryngoscopy. Cancer involves a visible mass, and a fixed cord with a mass on the same side is a T3 cancer until proven otherwise.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acid Reflux (GERD) / Laryngopharyngeal Reflux
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD-related throat symptoms include throat clearing, globus sensation, mild hoarseness, and bitter taste; laryngoscopy shows posterior laryngeal oedema and erythema, not a mass. Symptoms improve with acid suppression and lifestyle change within 8-12 weeks. Persistent hoarseness despite reflux treatment, or any visible laryngeal mass, requires biopsy to exclude cancer.

confusedWithConditions.name: Oral Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Oral cancer arises from the lips, tongue, floor of mouth, buccal mucosa, or hard palate and presents with a non-healing oral ulcer, pain on chewing, or visible lesion. Laryngeal cancer arises below the oral cavity and presents primarily with voice change, sore throat, or otalgia. Both share tobacco and alcohol as causes and can occur as synchronous or metachronous second primaries.

relatedConditions.name: Oral Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares tobacco and alcohol aetiology; oral cavity is the most common site of synchronous or metachronous second primary tumours in laryngeal cancer survivors.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Field cancerisation from tobacco gives laryngeal cancer patients a 10-20% lifetime risk of a lung primary; chest imaging is part of staging and surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal Cord Paralysis
relatedConditions.relevance: Often the first sign of bulky T3 laryngeal cancer or recurrent laryngeal nerve invasion; any new vocal cord paralysis without surgical cause warrants imaging of the entire nerve course.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Laryngitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Can mimic early glottic cancer; severe dysplasia within chronic laryngitis lesions progresses to invasive cancer in roughly 30% of cases over 5 years.

relatedConditions.name: Acid Reflux (GERD)
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-standing laryngopharyngeal reflux is a probable co-carcinogen for supraglottic disease and complicates post-treatment recovery; treated alongside cancer therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Vocal Cord Nodules
relatedConditions.relevance: Benign voice-use lesions that share the symptom of hoarseness; reliable laryngoscopy is what distinguishes them from early carcinoma.

faqs.question: What is the most common early sign of laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: The earliest and most common sign is persistent hoarseness or voice change lasting more than 3 weeks. Because the vocal cords are exquisitely sensitive to even small lesions, glottic tumours typically declare themselves while they are still curable. Any adult smoker or former smoker with hoarseness beyond 3 weeks should be referred for urgent ENT laryngoscopy rather than treated empirically for laryngitis.

faqs.question: Is laryngeal cancer curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — laryngeal cancer is highly curable when caught early. Five-year relative survival is approximately 78% for localised disease and exceeds 90% for T1 glottic cancer treated with either transoral laser surgery or radiation. Survival falls to roughly 47% for regional disease and 34% for distant disease, which is why early symptoms must be investigated quickly.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: SEER 2024 reports a 5-year relative survival of approximately 61% overall for laryngeal cancer: 78% for localised, 47% for regional, and 34% for distant disease. Glottic cancers do better stage-for-stage than supraglottic or subglottic primaries because they present earlier with hoarseness. Stage, subsite, smoking status during treatment, and access to multidisciplinary care drive the differences in outcome.

faqs.question: What causes laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: Cigarette smoking causes 70-80% of laryngeal cancers, and heavy alcohol use is a strong independent and synergistic risk factor; combined heavy smoking and drinking accounts for over 90% of cases in many series. Other contributors include occupational asbestos and chemical exposures, chronic laryngopharyngeal reflux, HPV in a small subset, and inherited DNA-repair disorders such as Fanconi anaemia.

faqs.question: How is laryngeal cancer diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with flexible laryngoscopy in the ENT clinic, followed by biopsy under general anaesthesia at panendoscopy to confirm tissue diagnosis and map the tumour. Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the neck and chest defines T-stage and nodal disease; PET/CT is used for stage III-IV disease. Every confirmed case is reviewed at a multidisciplinary head and neck oncology meeting before treatment is offered.

faqs.question: What are the three subsites of laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: The larynx is divided into supraglottis (epiglottis, false cords, aryepiglottic folds), glottis (true vocal cords), and subglottis (below the cords). Glottic cancers account for ~60% of cases and present early with hoarseness. Supraglottic cancers account for ~30-35% and often present with neck lymph node metastasis. Subglottic cancers account for under 5% and tend to present late with airway obstruction.

faqs.question: What is the difference between glottic and supraglottic cancer?
faqs.answer: Glottic cancer arises on the vocal cords, presents very early with hoarseness, has limited lymphatic drainage, and has the best stage-for-stage prognosis. Supraglottic cancer arises above the cords, presents later with sore throat, otalgia, or a neck lump, has rich bilateral lymphatic drainage, and produces nodal metastasis in 25-50% of patients at diagnosis. Treatment plans and elective neck dissection decisions differ accordingly.

faqs.question: How is early laryngeal cancer treated?
faqs.answer: Early laryngeal cancer (T1-T2 N0) is treated with single-modality therapy — transoral laser microsurgery, transoral robotic surgery, or definitive radiation therapy — with equivalent local control rates of 85-95%. The choice depends on tumour location, voice priorities, comorbidities, and centre expertise. Voice outcomes are generally similar between modalities; surgery preserves radiation as a salvage option, and vice versa.

faqs.question: What is total laryngectomy?
faqs.answer: Total laryngectomy is surgical removal of the entire larynx, creating a permanent breathing stoma in the neck. It is the standard for T4a tumours with cartilage destruction, dysfunctional larynx, or after failed larynx-preserving therapy. Voice rehabilitation with a tracheoesophageal voice prosthesis is achieved by 80-90% of motivated patients; electrolarynx and oesophageal speech are alternatives.

faqs.question: Can the larynx be preserved in advanced cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes — for many T3 cancers and selected T4 cancers, concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation preserves the larynx in 67% of patients at 10 years (RTOG 91-11). T4a tumours with bulky cartilage destruction or non-functional larynx have poor outcomes with non-surgical preservation and are typically offered upfront total laryngectomy with adjuvant therapy. The decision is made in a multidisciplinary tumour board with patient values weighted heavily.

faqs.question: What was the RTOG 91-11 trial?
faqs.answer: RTOG 91-11 was a landmark randomised trial of 547 patients with locally advanced laryngeal cancer that compared induction chemotherapy plus radiation, concurrent cisplatin-radiation, and radiation alone. Concurrent chemoradiation produced superior larynx preservation (84% at 2 years, 67% at 10 years) and locoregional control. The trial established concurrent cisplatin-radiation as a standard of care for larynx preservation.

faqs.question: How long does radiation treatment for laryngeal cancer take?
faqs.answer: A standard course of definitive radiotherapy delivers 66-70 Gy in 30-35 daily fractions over 6-7 weeks (typically Monday to Friday). Each daily appointment lasts 15-30 minutes including set-up. Acute side effects (sore throat, mucositis, fatigue) peak in weeks 4-6 and settle over 8-12 weeks after the last fraction.

faqs.question: Can I still talk after a total laryngectomy?
faqs.answer: Yes — most patients achieve fluent intelligible speech after total laryngectomy. The gold standard is a tracheoesophageal voice prosthesis inserted during or shortly after surgery, which 80-90% of motivated patients use successfully. Alternatives are oesophageal speech (swallowing air and releasing it through the upper oesophagus) and an electrolarynx device held against the neck.

faqs.question: Does HPV cause laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: HPV is identified in only 5-15% of laryngeal cancers, far less than the >70% rate in oropharyngeal cancer. The prognostic benefit seen in HPV-driven oropharyngeal cancer does not consistently apply to the larynx. HPV-6 and HPV-11 cause recurrent respiratory papillomatosis, which has a small lifetime risk of malignant transformation. HPV vaccination is expected to reduce both oropharyngeal and recurrent respiratory papillomatosis-related cancers over the coming decades.

faqs.question: Does smoking after diagnosis affect my outcome?
faqs.answer: Yes — continued smoking during radiotherapy reduces response rates by 30-40%, doubles the risk of a second primary cancer, and roughly halves long-term survival (Browman NEJM 1993). Smoking cessation at diagnosis is the single most powerful patient-controlled intervention. Every head and neck oncology unit should offer formal cessation support, nicotine replacement, or varenicline at diagnosis.

faqs.question: What are the side effects of laryngeal cancer treatment?
faqs.answer: Common side effects include mucositis, dysphagia, weight loss, fatigue, xerostomia, taste change, skin reaction, hoarseness, and tinnitus or hearing loss with cisplatin. Long-term effects include chronic xerostomia, dental decay, hypothyroidism (in up to 50% within 5 years of neck irradiation), lymphoedema, and risk of osteoradionecrosis. Total laryngectomy adds permanent stoma care and the need for voice rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Can laryngeal cancer come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — local or regional recurrence occurs in 15-30% of patients within 5 years, most often within the first 2 years. Distant metastases (lung most common) develop in 15-25% of advanced cases. Surveillance with clinical exam, laryngoscopy, and cross-sectional imaging is intensive in years 1-2 and tapers thereafter. Salvage surgery offers a curative option for isolated local recurrence in many cases.

faqs.question: How much does laryngeal cancer treatment cost?
faqs.answer: In the United States, total billed cost for stage III-IV laryngeal cancer commonly ranges from USD 80,000 to USD 250,000 across surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and rehabilitation; insurance covers most costs for in-network multidisciplinary care. In India and other emerging markets, comprehensive treatment in a high-volume cancer centre typically ranges from INR 4 to 12 lakh (USD 5,000 to 15,000). Voice prosthesis maintenance is an ongoing yearly cost.

faqs.question: Is laryngeal cancer hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most laryngeal cancer is sporadic and driven by tobacco and alcohol rather than inheritance. Rare inherited DNA-repair disorders, particularly Fanconi anaemia, increase head and neck SCC risk 500-700-fold and often present before age 40. Common genetic variants in alcohol and DNA-repair genes modify individual risk modestly. Family history of head and neck cancer doubles personal risk and warrants earlier symptom-led assessment.

faqs.question: Can women get laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes — women account for roughly 20-25% of US cases, and the gap is closing in countries where female smoking rose in recent decades. Female laryngeal cancer patients tend to be older at diagnosis, have a higher proportion of supraglottic disease, and historically have had inferior stage-adjusted survival linked to delayed referral. Persistent hoarseness should be investigated as urgently in women as in men.

faqs.question: What is the role of immunotherapy in laryngeal cancer?
faqs.answer: Immune checkpoint inhibitors targeting PD-1 (pembrolizumab and nivolumab) are now standard for recurrent or metastatic head and neck SCC. Pembrolizumab — alone in PD-L1 CPS ≥1 disease or combined with platinum-fluorouracil regardless of CPS — is first-line based on KEYNOTE-048 (Burtness Lancet 2019). Nivolumab is approved for platinum-refractory disease. Trials are evaluating immunotherapy in the curative-intent setting alongside chemoradiation.

faqs.question: What is the follow-up schedule after laryngeal cancer treatment?
faqs.answer: NCCN recommends review every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, every 4-8 months in years 3-5, and annually thereafter. Each visit includes clinical exam, laryngoscopy, and a review of voice, swallowing, and pain. Chest imaging is performed at least annually given the elevated lung primary risk in smokers; PET/CT and MRI are used selectively. TSH is checked annually after neck irradiation.
